sentences.text: Yeah.
That is correct.
These musicians played every day, and rain or shine, there were out there playing.
And they-- when we-- as I said, we were-- we were taught how to lay bricks.
And we were brought to this kommando-- [GERMAN], which was a factory that was being built for the [GERMAN].
But when we got to the work place, we found out that we were not actually supposed to lay the bricks.
The bricklayers we Polish workers.
Polish citizens.
Civilians, that lived around the area of Auschwitz.
They came from the city of Auschwitz itself, and from Katowice, which is nearby Auschwitz.
And some even as far away as Krakow, which is not too far away.
And they worked for the Germans.
And they got paid.
And what our job was, to hand them the mortar and to mix the mortar for them.
And to hand it to them as they were laying the bricks.
Of course, again, we were outside.
And we were working.
And we were exposed to the cold and to the rain and everything.
Well, it-- it so happened that-- yeah, I want to point out that these civilian workers that worked there, they were all Polish citizens.
And they got paid by the Germans.
But they got paid with German money-- with this-- this occupation money.
Not German money, occupation money.
And this money, of course, had no value.
And they couldn't buy anything for it.
Just the official rations.
So they had no-- they couldn't buy, for instance, a shirt, or a pair of pants, or a sweater.
That they were not able to buy.
Because that was just not available to them.
But we, in the-- in the camp, we had warehouses in the camp where all the clothing that was stored in there, that was taken away from the people that came into the camp.
And the people, the unfortunate people that were gassed right away, they took all their clothes and all their belongings.
And they took all those belongings from those people.
And they sorted them out.
They had a kommando, which we called Kanada.
Kanada had a big-- we called it Kanada because Kanada had a vision for us that that was a large country that had everything.
So we called the people that worked in that kommando, Kanada.
And I had a friend.
Klein was his name.
A young fellow, same age as I was.
He got a job in Kanada.
Now when you worked in that kommando, of course, you had all the food you wanted.
Because the people that came in brought food along with them.
Some of them, anyway.
And they could eat that food while they were working there.
But they could not take it out.
So what this fellow would do-- he would take a sweater, or a pair of pants, and put it on underneath his uniform.
And smuggle it into the camp.
And when he was inside the camp, he would give it to me.
And I would take it back out again.
I would also put it on under my uniform, which was, incidentally, a very risky thing to do, because if you were caught with that, there was a death penalty for that-- by hanging.
Because they said that you were trying to escape.
You had civilian clothing on, and that means that you were trying to escape-- you were making a plan to escape.
So I took the risk.
We had to take that risk because the food that they gave us, officially, was not enough to sustain you.
So we had to find ways of helping ourselves.
So I took that clothing, put it on underneath my uniform, brought it out, and we sold it to those civilians-- those Polish civilians that were working there laying bricks.
Now, we couldn't sell it to them for money, because money was of no value.
It was not to us, especially, because we couldn't use it at all.
We sold it for food, for bread, for a piece of kielbasa, or cigarettes, which was a big commodity-- cigarettes.
And we took that back to the camp.
And that's how we had a little black market.
And were able to help ourselves to survive.
Some of those civilians, incidentally, were very bad.
They were anti-Semitic.
And sometimes they used to report you.
They used to take the clothing from you.
And then they used to call over the guard and say, hey, this Jew wants to try to sell me this sweater or a shirt or something like that.
And that unfortunate guy, that was the end of him.
But I was fortunate enough.
I was never caught.
And I managed to supplement my ration that way.
So as we were working along on this factory, the factory was completed.
They worked very fast on that factory.
They wanted to do it.
They wanted to complete it in a very big hurry.
Because the Kruppwerke, who manufactured ammunitions, had gone into Russia.
See, the German army had advanced all the way into Russia.
So what the Germans did in order to shorten their supply lines-- there was a long distance.
They went almost up all the way to Stalingrad.
From Stalingrad back to Germany was a very long distance.
So the supply-- to shorten their supply lines, they built factories right behind the front lines.
And they had prisoners working in those factories.
They were manufacturing ammunitions for the army.
So as the Russian army, though, started to push the Germans back, they took that factory, and they loaded it up on flat railroad cars.
All the machinery loaded on flat railroad cars.
And shipped that factory back to Auschwitz, where we had-- where we were building that factory there.
But, on the way over there, the Russian air force bombarded that train.
And all that machinery was-- was damaged, severely damaged.
All burned and broken.
So when the train arrived in Auschwitz, they wanted to unload the train and start up the factory.
But the machinery was useless.
So they were looking for schlossers-- mechanics, toolmakers.
And everybody wanted to be a toolmaker or a mechanic, because that meant that he could work inside that factory, where it was warm.
And again, you were protected from the elements.
So they were looking for these mechanics to fix this machinery, so that they could produce munitions again.
But they got wise.
The Germans got wise to us, that everybody wanted to work in the factory, that were not even mechanics, that didn't know anything about it.
So they gave you a test.
The test consisted of-- that-- yeah-- in that factory, to open the factory, you see, they had sent from Germany-- they sent civilian engineers-- meisters.
They call them meister over there.
Meister is an engineer-- mechanical engineer.
And they were supervising the work inside the factory.
The guards, of course, were the SS.
And the meisters were supervising the work.
They had the experience, of the knowledge, of what to do, see.
So they gave us a test.
The test consisted of this-- they gave you a piece of metal.
Put it in a vise, and they handed you a file.
And they said, here, take that and file it.
So they would see how you would handle this tool.
They would see if you were really a mechanic or not.
So I had a friend in Auschwitz that was a mechanic.
And he told me-- he said, look, you have to try to get into that factory, because that's going to save your life.
And I'll show you what to do.
And he did.
He showed me how to hold the file, and how to do this.
And I could not tell them that I was a mechanic.
Because I was very young.
By that time, I was about 17, 18 years old.
So I told him that I was an apprentice-- an apprentice schlosser.
That's mechanic, in Germany.
And it worked.
I did this test with the file, and they accepted me to work.
And I worked in that factory in the machine shop.
They had a large machine shop.
We had 30 mechanics in the machine shop with lathes and-- and milling machines, and all-- all the machinery.
But the machinery that they-- that we unloaded from those trains was worthless.
They could not repair them.
It was too far gone.
So they had to junk the whole thing.
And they brought in a new factory-- a factory called [NON-ENGLISH].
This factory came from Wuppertal, Germany-- it's in-- it's in southern Germany in-- in the-- near the the southern Germany and Bayern, In the province of-- of Bavaria.
And this factory-- before the war, they manufactured bicycles.
But during the war now, they manufactured a-- a spark plug for an anti-tank weapon, which was the size of about this cup here.
Like, this large.
Which-- this-- this spark plug was put into an anti-tank-- anti-tank weapon which was thrown at a tank, and blew up the tank, then.
And what they manufactured in that factory was just that spark plug.
So I was fortunate enough to get selected to work in there.
And like I said, I worked in a machine shop, which saved my life.
Because we worked inside, then.
And I worked in that factory for quite some time-- for almost a year and a half, I would say.
Now, there were some incidents that-- the one incident was that they were-- there were women working in the factory, also.
There were about 2,000 people working in that factory.
You can imagine how big it was.
The meisters-- the engineers-- most of them, I would say, 98% of them, were huge anti-Semites.
They were all party members.
Members of the Nazi party.
They all wore a large swastika on their shirt.
And they were not in sympathy to us.
Now, as I said, there were women working in there, too.
I would say there were about 800 women and 1,200 men.
They did not work together-- you know, close together.
They women worked in one section of the factory, and the men worked in another section of the factory.
There was also a section in the factory there where they manufactured the shell for this-- for this spark plug.
And that-- that shell had to be drawn in a-- from a-- from a hot furnace, red.
There were people working there without shirts, naked-- half-naked-- with huge tongs.
They would pick up this metal, put it in there, and then take it out.
It was very hard work.
Even the Nazis in the-- in the factory said that these workers should get a special ration.
There, in the kitchen, while they were working, they could eat whatever they wanted, when they we were working there.
But they could not bring anything out of the kitchen.
Now, they were making, like I said-- they were making sausages.
Now these sausages, they were cooked in a huge kettle.
See, they made the sausage, and then they took the raw meat.
And put them into that kettle.
And then they cooked them in there.
And that water, usually, is thrown out.
Because that's just greasy water.
But they decided that that greasy water is a double ration for those people that worked so hard.
So once a week, I was assigned to go to that kitchen with a wagon and two horses-- a wagon-- a wagon-- is a wagon that was just with four wheels, and there was just some boards thrown over it.
Had no springs or anything.
They called it a [NON-ENGLISH] wagon in Polish.
And I got a permit to go, because I was a young guy and I spoke German.
And they let me do this.

And I had to go to that kitchen, every week, once a week, with two kettles-- army Army kettles, thermos kettles, you know, that get sealed, like they have in the army, where they bring the food for the soldiers.
And I had to bring those kettles to the kitchen.
I was not allowed to go inside.
I had to knock on the door.
An SS guard came out and saw who it was.
He called out the butchers there, the prisoners that were working there.
They took the two kettles inside, they filled them up, and then they brought them out.
And he helped me put them back on the wagon.
And then I went back.

So we-- and that's the water-- that water they gave to these unfortunate people that were working so hard on those hot presses, where they had to-- with hot-- with tongs, they had to bring out the hot metal that was fire-- fire red.
Well, we wanted to figure out a way-- how could we bring out some of those sausages from that kitchen.
So we had a meeting in the camp, with the butchers.
And we devised a plan.
In the factory where I worked, in the machine shop, we also had a tinsmith shop.
The tinsmith shop manufactured a double bottom for those kettles.
And we put-- when I brought the kettles to the kitchen, the butchers put some sausages into the-- into the bottom.
And then put the false bottom on top of it.
In this way, I was able to bring that back to the factory there.
And this went on for quite a while.
Until, one day, another fellow-- Max was his name-- he said to me, I want to go with you.
Because you're also going to the camp.
This was another job that I had to do.
At the same time that I went to the kitchen, I also had to stop by the camp, by the main gate of the camp.
And I had to give them sheets of all the names of the people that worked in the factory.
Every day somebody had to go back to the camp, incidentally.
I had to do it once a week.
The day that I went to the kitchen, I had to go and bring back the report on how many people were in the factory.
The factory, incidentally, worked on a 24 hour tour.
We worked from 7 o'clock in the morning until 7 o'clock at night.
And then the next tour came in at 7 o'clock, and worked until 7 o'clock in the morning.
As one tour walked out, the other tour walked in.
So the factory kept on working around the clock.
The reason I had to bring back the sheets of the names of people that worked inside the factory was because the-- the camp got paid for every worker that worked in the factory.
We never saw the money, of course.
We never got the money.
The SS guards, they got the money-- the one that guarded us.
They got the money.
They got the payment for us.
I don't know how much money it was, but they got paid for every man that worked.
Well, anyway, that particular day, this Max, he wanted to come with me.
I said to him-- and this Max, nobody trusted him.
Because we knew that he was too friendly with the meisters.
And he was too friendly with the guards.
So we did not trust this guy.
So I was very leery about him.
And I told him, right away, I said, look, you cannot come with me, because, look, you don't have a pass.
And if they get caught on the way, you're in trouble.
They're going to say that you were trying to run away.
So he says, I have a pass.
And he did.
Somehow he got a pass.
Well, anyway, we got to the kitchen.
We dropped off the kettles, like we always did.
Knocked on the door.
The butcher came out.
He took it inside.
Now he came out with the kettles.
And the guard was there-- the SS guard.
And we were just about to lift the kettle and put them on the wagon.
He said, halt!
Halt!
What's now?
He took a broom-- a broom.
And he took a broom upside down, and he said to the butcher, he says, open up the kettle.
And he took this broom and he started to stir the kettle inside, the water-- the hot water that was in there, that greasy water.
He didn't see nothing.
So he took the stick-- took the stick out again, and he measured the outside of the kettle, and he measured the inside of the kettle.
And while he was doing that, Max disappeared.
He went away.
He ran off.
And I was standing there with my heart in my pants, because I knew what was in there.
But nothing happened.
He said, close it up.
Raus!
And we had to take the kettle, put them on the wagon.
And I ran back with the horses, back to the camp.
We unloaded the kettles.
And I told them what had happened.
And we were all scared.
We didn't know what really happened.
They didn't catch us.
At night when we got back to the camp, I went up to the block where the butchers were.
They were on a different barrack, different block.
And I asked them what happened.
So they told me that they found out that somebody squealed on us.
And they told them that we have a false bottom.
They were able to take that bottom out and hide it.
So this way, we were not caught.
It was very fortunate.
Well, there was another thing that-- another incident that I want to mention in Auschwitz, that was the-- they were the women that were working in there.
Now, there was a group of women that were working in a bunker that was outside the factory-- just a little bit away from the factory.
There was a bunker halfway underground.
In that bunker they stored the dynamite that was used in that spark plug.
See, the spark plug had to have a small amount of dynamite, which was-- which ignited on impact, and then exploded this whole thing and blew up the tank.
So this dynamite was stored in that bunker.
And the women that were working in there, they had to take out just enough dynamite for every tour-- for every day tour to bring into the factory.
Because they didn't want to store all this dynamite inside the factory.
It was dangerous.
So the women had this dynamite.
Now, then, there was another kommando-- which was called the Sonderkommando.
The Sonderkommando was a kommando that worked in the crematoria where they burned all the bodies that were gassed.
And these people, what they did-- the-- the SS took these people that worked there, about 400 of them.
The kommando consisted of 400 prisoners.
Every once in a while, they would take all these people and kill them.
Because they did not-- and put new ones in there-- new prisoners.
Because they did not want them to know how many people were killed.
You see, because they saw that on a daily basis.
So this particular Sonderkommando that was there at that time-- that was already in 1944, that happened-- they knew that their time was coming, that they were going to kill them.
And they wanted to make an uprising.
They wanted to blow up this crematoria.
There were all together three of them-- three or four of those crematorias.
I think there was three or four.
I don't even remember anymore.
But anyway, they wanted to blow that up.
And they wanted to make an uprising.
And they contacted the Polish resistance that was on the outside-- civilian Polish resistance, for help, for arms.
But they could get-- couldn't get any help from them.
So finally, they knew, that in that factory-- where I worked, and where the women were working with that dynamite-- they knew that there was dynamite there.
So they contacted these women and they asked them to smuggle out dynamite, to bring it out into the camp, so that they could use that dynamite.
Now, the only way-- they couldn't take it in packages and take it out.
They would have caught them right away.
So what they did, they did it in the seam of their clothing.
Every day, they put a little bit of this dynamite, and hid it inside the clothing.
And they brought it out that way, until one day, they had enough dynamite that they felt they had enough to blow up this crematoria.
And they actually did.
They blew up the crematoria and the gas chamber.
And they killed several of the guards, of course.
But all these 400 guys that worked in that kommando, they were all killed.
But they did manage to blow up the crematoria and the gas chamber.
And that was done with the help of these women that worked there.
Now, the SS guards knew that the only place where they could have gotten this dynamite was from our factory.
So they took these girls-- there was four of them.
They tortured them terribly.
But they would not reveal whom they gave the dynamite to.
But, finally, they took these four girls and they hung them, in front of the entire factory.
We had to watch them hanging.
Two girls-- on was Esther, and one was Rosa.
The other one, the other two I don't remember the names, offhand.
But two were hanged in the morning tour, and two were hanged in the evening tour.
So that everybody was able to watch them.
Their faces were unrecognizable from the tortures that they received from the interrogations.
And it was only now that the Auschwitz committee was able to get permission from Yad Vashem.
It was now in-- 19-- I believe it was a few years ago, in 1989, I think it was.
They erected a monument in Yad Vashem for those four girls.
We-- we collected-- we all contributed money to get a-- a architect, a stonemason, to make a monument of those four girls-- those four heroes.
And that monument is now in Yad Vashem.
Yeah.
Then-- we-- then-- soon-- this incident happened in-- in 1944.
I believe it was in October of 1944.
And a few months later, in January of 1944, the Russian army in the east had advanced so far that he could hear, already, the artillery shells bursting.
We heard the-- the-- you know, we heard the shooting in the distance.
And they decided to evacuate Auschwitz.
So on January 18, 1945, Auschwitz was evacuated.
And we started to march.
They put us in groups of 1,000, and marched us out west.
Now, this was, mind you, in January.
It was very cold.
And we marched for four days.
We slept at night in the fields, in open fields.
Anybody that fell behind and was weak was shot immediately.
And their bodies were just left on the roadside.
The whole road was full of dead bodies.
You could see, as we were marching along, from the columns that were walking ahead of us-- people that got shot were just left laying on the road side.
